MRS. STAFFORD


REPLIES TO MAYOR

pages.raw_text:
letters follow; they are the result
Of the weeks work of committee
of women in investigating Jail con-
ditions and the local social evil, and
they bear the defy direct to the
public officials who have been
laughing at the efforts of the Wom-
ans club to get clean Jail condi-
tons.


The letter of Mrs. Stafford to
Mayor Pratt follows:


IN. S. Pratt, Mayor of Spokane
In the Spokesman-Review of Febru-
airy 25 you are quoted as saying:
If Mrs. Stafford is a good citizen,
she will come forth with her proofs.


"And in the Inland Herald of Feb
ruary "a you are quoted as saying:
The police force is worthy the con-
fidence of the people. and it is a
shame that it should be subjected
to wanton attacks backed up by
nothing better than I. w. w. affi-
davits.''


II first wish to assure you, Mayor
Pratt, of my good citizenship. My
idea of good citizenship is as far
removed from your own as the
north pole is from the south pole
You promised the people, before
your election, non-partisan. re-
form government. Has your citizen
ship enabled you to keep your
word?


''The mayor of city is supposed
to know at least something of all
departments of the city govern
ment. On the floor of the council
February 90, Councilman Mohr
made the statement that Chief Sui
Ivan was but figurehead and that
Mayor Pratt was the real chief of
police If this be true, and you
have never denied It, have you?
then you must be familiar with the
inner workings of your police de-
partment.


"And when the guilt is fixed you
must bear your share.


THE affidavits which substan-
tiate my charges of conditions in
the city Jail are ready for inspec-
ton. We have used no I. w. w.
affidavits. Your statement regard
ing our affidavits was, we'll, let us
say. ungentlemanly.


mo one wants good government,
no one longs for right conditions to
prevail more than does the Wom-
ans club. It's aim is educational and
social. If the Woman's club can
educate the public mind about the
true conditions of affairs, then its
good citizenship is proven, and the
individuals of the club who work
out this educational line prove their
good citizenship. Right is not al
ways pleasant thing to fight for:
for principle one must sometimes
stand the opprobrium of public opin-
ion. But. Mayor Pratt. public opin-
ion is tickle thing, and back Of it
is the spirit of real American men
and women-the spirit of '76. And
If we can only get beneath the cur
rent commercialism to the fighting
snirit inherent in us all the truth
of principles that stand for decency,
for honesty and good government.
then will public opinion become a
power before which all must give
way.


The Woman's club has waited for
seven days for official notice from
the city clerk to appoint a member
